Question: Which of the following passages is the original verbatim passage? The other passages are paraphrased versions of it.

Options:
A. On March 17, 2023, the French National Assembly received two separate motions expressing a lack of confidence in the minority government led by Prime Minister Élisabeth Borne. A multi-party coalition, encompassing the left-leaning NUPES and the regionalist LIOT, put forward one of these motions; Bertrand Pancher initially introduced it, and Charles de Courson subsequently presented its defense. The second motion originated from the right-wing populist National Rally, with Marine Le Pen introducing it and Laure Lavalette defending its contents. Both of these motions were submitted as a direct consequence of the government's decision to invoke constitutional article 49.3. This particular article was utilized to enact a contentious piece of legislation that mandated an increase in the retirement age from 62 to 64 years. On March 20, both motions underwent a vote and were ultimately defeated. The cross-party motion, in particular, was rejected by a narrow difference of only 9 votes, representing the smallest margin of failure recorded since 1990. B. Two motions of no confidence in the minority government of Prime Minister Élisabeth Borne were tabled on 17 March 2023 in the French National Assembly. One motion was proposed by a cross-party alliance that included the left-wing NUPES and the regionalist LIOT (introduced by Bertrand Pancher and defended by Charles de Courson); the other by the right-wing populist National Rally (introduced by Marine Le Pen and defended by Laure Lavalette). The two motions were introduced in response to the government's use of constitutional article 49.3 to pass a controversial law that raised the retirement age from 62 to 64. On 20 March, both motions were voted down. The cross-party motion failed by a margin of 9 votes, the slimmest since 1990.The National Rally (RN) voted in favour of
C. On 17 March 2023, the French National Assembly voted down two motions of no confidence in the minority government of Prime Minister Élisabeth Borne. The cross-party alliance, made up of left-wing NUPES and regionalist LIOT (introduced by Bertrand Pancher and defended by Charles de Courson), and right-wing populist National Rally (introduced by Marine Le Pen and defended by Laure Lavalette), both motions were defeated by 9 votes on 20 March.
